stories.headline: Avalanche reacquire Stanley Cup champion Nazem Kadri in trade with Calgary Flames
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche finalized a deal to bring back center Nazem Kadri from the Calgary Flames just before Friday's NHL trade deadline, reuniting the 35-year-old with the team he helped win the Stanley Cup in 2022. The Flames will retain 20% of Kadri's contract, giving him a $5.6 million cap hit with Colorado for the remainder of his deal, along with a 2027 fourth-round pick going to the Avalanche.

In return, Colorado sent forward Victor Olofsson, prospect Max Curran, a conditional 2028 first-round pick (reported as top-10 protected), and a conditional 2027 second-round pick to Calgary. Kadri posted 12 goals and 41 points in 61 games this season with the Flames, though his 6.9% shooting percentage is well below his career mark of 10.8%.

Kadri left Colorado via free agency after the 2022 championship due to cap constraints. His return gives the Avalanche extraordinary depth at center alongside Nathan MacKinnon, Brock Nelson, Nicolas Roy and Jack Drury. The addition is the capstone of a deadline push that also netted defensemen Nick Blankenburg and Brett Kulak.

stories.headline: Contaminated gasoline incident traced to open valve at Sinclair terminal in Henderson; state levies $365,694 fine
stories.summary:
State investigators determined that the contamination of more than 1.6 million gallons of gasoline sold across the Denver metro in January was caused by a valve mistakenly left open at HF Sinclair's fuel distribution terminal in Henderson. Workers failed to close the valve on Jan. 7, allowing diesel fuel to mix with regular unleaded gasoline; the error was not discovered until the morning of Jan. 8 after gas station operators reported irregularities.

Approximately 365,694 gallons of contaminated fuel were sold to 49 gas stations across the Front Range, including locations operated by King Soopers, Costco and Murphy Express. More than 1,000 consumers reported vehicle problems including stalling, sputtering and illuminated check engine lights after refueling.

Colorado's Division of Oil and Public Safety fined Sinclair $365,694 — calculated at $1 per gallon of contaminated gasoline — after working with the state Attorney General's office to exceed the standard $500-per-violation statutory cap. The company has implemented a two-person verification system and checklist for valve operations. Investigators also noted Sinclair did not notify state regulators until Jan. 9 and did not provide a full list of affected stations until Jan. 13.

stories.headline: Snowstorm brings 3–5 inches to Denver, triggers highway closures and school delays across Front Range
stories.summary:
A winter storm brought the first significant snowfall to Denver in 40 days on Friday, with the National Weather Service issuing a Winter Weather Advisory through 8 p.m. and forecasting 3 to 5 inches of accumulation in the city. Western and southern portions of the metro were expected to see 4 to 6 inches, while mountain areas along Interstate 70 faced up to 14 inches of fresh snow.

The storm disrupted travel across the region, forcing temporary closures of Interstate 25 northbound at the Wyoming border, U.S. 287 between Laporte and Wyoming, and eastbound U.S. 36 between Estes Park and Boulder due to crashes. Denver International Airport issued a ground stop, and numerous school districts and government offices announced closures or delays.

Temperatures hovered near freezing for most of Friday, roughly 19 degrees below the seasonal average, with snowfall expected to taper off by 8 p.m. A warming trend is forecast to follow through the weekend and into next week, with highs returning to the low 70s by Monday.

stories.headline: Cherry Hills Village officer shoots car theft suspect inside Englewood hospital after attack
stories.summary:
A Cherry Hills Village police officer shot and injured a car theft suspect Thursday evening inside HCA HealthONE Swedish Medical Center in Englewood after the suspect violently attacked the officer during a medical examination. The officer had been monitoring the suspect, who had been arrested earlier in the day on suspicion of vehicle theft, while the person received treatment at the hospital.

The shooting occurred just after 5:30 p.m. and no other patients, staff or visitors were injured. The suspect remains hospitalized and has not been publicly identified. The officer was placed on paid administrative leave per department policy.

Cherry Hills Village Police Chief Jason Lyons said the incident underscores why officers remain with suspects during medical care. The 18th Judicial District will investigate the shooting.

stories.headline: Denver police chase ends on I-70 in Aurora after suspect in stolen vehicle runs over officer's foot
stories.summary:
A Denver police pursuit of a stolen vehicle ended Thursday evening on eastbound Interstate 70 in Aurora, snarling rush-hour traffic for nearly three hours. The chase began near 45th Avenue and Zuni Street after the driver of the stolen vehicle fled a traffic stop and ran over an officer's foot.

Officers pursued the suspect and performed a vehicle stop on eastbound I-70 west of Chambers Road. The highway was fully closed at Interstate 225 from 4:51 p.m. until about 7:30 p.m., with one lane reopening around 6:15 p.m.

The suspect was arrested and transported to a hospital for evaluation, as was the officer whose foot was injured. The Colorado Department of Transportation managed traffic diversions throughout the closure.

stories.headline: U.S. House committee probes fraud and waste in Colorado's Medicaid program, sets March 17 response deadline
stories.summary:
The U.S. House Committee on Energy and Commerce has sent a formal inquiry to Gov. Jared Polis and the Colorado Department of Health Care Policy and Finance, demanding information about the state's Medicaid oversight practices by March 17. Committee Chair Rep. Brett Guthrie cited concerns that Colorado's program may be vulnerable to the same fraud patterns found in Minnesota, including falsified records, identity theft and phantom billing claims.

The letter highlighted a 436% spike in non-emergency medical transportation spending between 2019 and 2025, attributing much of it to incorrect state guidance that misidentified patients as needing specialty ambulances — an error regulators say has since been corrected and could save $60.5 million in fiscal year 2026-27. Investigators also flagged potential fraud in applied behavioral analysis services for children with autism and genetic laboratory billing, with fraudulent transport claims alone estimated at more than $25 million.

Colorado Medicaid spending has surpassed $14.6 billion, nearly doubling in a decade despite enrollment growing just 7%. Gov. Polis, through a spokesperson, said the state takes Medicaid oversight seriously and is reviewing the committee's letter. The U.S. Attorney for Colorado filed criminal charges against two individuals related to the transportation billing issues last month.

stories.headline: Denver Public Schools board considers policy creating safe zones, restricting staff cooperation with ICE
stories.summary:
Denver Public Schools' board of education held a community hearing Thursday on a proposed policy that would designate all DPS properties — including buildings, grounds, bus stops and school-sponsored events — as safe zones for students, families and employees. Under the policy, school staff would be prohibited from complying with ICE detainer requests, and school resource officers would be barred from sharing student or family information with federal immigration enforcement.

Supporters, including students and immigrant advocacy groups, said fear of immigration enforcement is causing measurable harm to students' mental health and school attendance. Several students testified about anxiety triggered by what they described as ICE presence near school facilities.

Opponents at the meeting argued the district should not shield individuals from federal law enforcement and that the policy creates a harmful environment. DPS is Colorado's largest school district, serving tens of thousands of students, many from immigrant families.

stories.headline: Measles outbreak in Colorado grows to eight cases; exposure sites span DIA, schools, grocery stores and bowling alley
stories.summary:
Colorado's measles outbreak has grown to eight confirmed cases in 2026, all linked to connections with Broomfield High School and Broomfield Heights Middle School, according to the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment. The state has identified 20 possible exposure sites across Broomfield, Denver, Frederick, Lafayette, Louisville and Westminster.

Exposure locations include Denver International Airport, multiple restaurants, health care facilities, a grocery store, a church and a bowling alley. Health officials warn that measles symptoms — including fever, cough, runny nose and red eyes — can take up to three weeks to appear, and infected individuals can spread the virus before they develop the characteristic rash.

Colorado recorded 36 measles cases in 2025, a sharp increase over prior years, which had typically seen zero to two cases annually. Two doses of the MMR vaccine provide roughly 97% protection; partial protection is possible if the vaccine is administered within three days of exposure.

stories.headline: Federal judge dismisses homebuilder's constitutional challenge to Denver affordable housing fees
stories.summary:
A federal judge dismissed a lawsuit Tuesday filed by Denver-area homebuilder redT Homes and affiliated company redT Capital Partners against the city of Denver over its affordable housing linkage fee ordinance. U.S. District Court Judge Philip A. Brimmer granted Denver's motion to dismiss, finding that the city's mandatory affordable housing ordinance provided a constitutional alternative method of compliance for developers.

The plaintiffs had argued that linkage fees of approximately $25,000 and $45,000 imposed on two small residential developments constituted an unconstitutional government taking, claiming the fees lacked proportionality to the developments' actual impact on the housing shortage. Judge Brimmer agreed that proportionality would ordinarily need to be explored but concluded the availability of alternative compliance options made the ordinance constitutional.

Denver's linkage fee ordinance requires developers of smaller projects to pay a per-square-foot fee into the city's affordable housing fund or designate units as affordable housing, based on the city council's findings that new development drives job growth and increases demand for workforce housing. The ruling preserves a key component of Denver's affordable housing strategy.

stories.headline: Nikola Jokic posts triple-double as Nuggets survive Lakers' late rally, 120–113
stories.summary:
Nikola Jokic recorded 28 points, 12 rebounds and 13 assists — despite nine turnovers — to lead the Denver Nuggets past the Los Angeles Lakers 120-113 on Thursday at Ball Arena. Jokic hit a crucial 8-footer with 22.3 seconds remaining to seal the win after the Lakers, the NBA's most clutch-time team, threatened to tie the game in the final minutes.

Jamal Murray matched Jokic with 28 points, while Julian Strawther added 18 in his continued starting role necessitated by injuries. Christian Braun played a team-high 40 minutes and held Luka Doncic — who finished with 27 points on 24 shots — largely in check on key possessions. Denver played without four regular rotation players: Peyton Watson, Aaron Gordon, Spencer Jones and Cam Johnson.

The Nuggets improved to 39-24 and maintained a 1.5-game lead over Los Angeles for fifth place in the Western Conference. LeBron James, who briefly left with an injury, returned late and passed Kareem Abdul-Jabbar for the most field goals made in NBA history with a first-quarter baseline jumper.

stories.headline: Broncos enter free agency with $28 million in cap space, targeting linebacker, running back and tight end
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos enter the 2026 NFL free agency period — with legal tampering beginning Monday and contracts signable Wednesday — carrying approximately $28 million in cap room, placing them in the top third of all teams. Team leadership has framed the offseason with the phrase "opportunistically aggressive," with quarterback Bo Nix under contract for two more seasons before a major extension decision looms.

Denver's most pressing needs include a linebacker, where the organization has a "significant appetite" according to sources, as well as a true lead running back to complement second-year player RJ Harvey. The team also needs a tight end and potentially a wide receiver to give Bo Nix additional weapons in 2026.

The running back market could center on Kenneth Walker III of Seattle, previously identified as a Broncos target, or Travis Etienne. The team is also weighing whether to trade backup quarterback Jarrett Stidham to free up $6.5 million in additional cap space, which would reshape their quarterback room depth.

stories.headline: Colorado State Patrol releases preliminary findings on fatal February dust storm crash involving 30-plus vehicles near Pueblo
stories.summary:
The Colorado State Patrol has released an updated preliminary report on the Feb. 17 multi-vehicle crash on Interstate 25 near Pueblo, which involved more than 30 vehicles and resulted in multiple fatalities and injuries. Investigators determined the chain-reaction crash began when a 2016 Ford Escape rear-ended a 2011 GMC Sierra hauling a trailer that was traveling 15-20 mph in a 75 mph zone during "brown-out" dust storm conditions.

After the initial collision, a Kenworth W900 hauling a trailer was sideswiped by a Ram 3500 whose stock trailer became detached. That Ram continued onto the shoulder and struck the Sierra, creating a blockage on I-25 that triggered a cascade of additional collisions involving numerous other vehicles.

CSP said the investigation is ongoing and the full timeline of events for several vehicles involved has not yet been established. The agency indicated it will release further updates as they become available.

weather.notable: About 12 degrees below the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: The only marathon I'll ever run is a Netflix marathon. And even then, I'll need a break halfway through.

weekendEvents.title: Denver Museum of Nature & Science Free Admission
weekendEvents.description: The Denver Museum of Nature & Science offers free admission during this SCFD free evening. No ticket purchase required for entry during this window.
weekendEvents.location: Denver Museum of Nature & Science, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Denver Restaurant Week
weekendEvents.description: The 22nd annual Denver Restaurant Week features multi-course meals at more than 300 participating restaurants across the metro, with fixed-price menus ranging from $25 to $55.

weekendEvents.title: Colorado Golf Expo
weekendEvents.description: A family-friendly golf expo featuring pro demos, new gear, swing tips, and mini golf. Free parking included with admission; kids are free.

weekendEvents.title: Mile High Beer Fest
weekendEvents.description: More than 40 local and national breweries, including Left Hand Brewing Co., Renegade Brewing, and Eddyline Brewery, pour unlimited samples at Denargo Market.

weekendEvents.title: SeriesFest Fashion in Focus
weekendEvents.description: Emmy-winning costume designer Molly Rogers, known for 'Sex and the City,' 'And Just Like That…,' and 'The Devil Wears Prada,' receives the Excellence in Costume Design Award alongside a runway show featuring more than 40 models and iconic historic costumes. Tickets start at $125 and include a cocktail party and the full fashion show.
weekendEvents.location: The Cable Center, University of Denver, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Blarney on Belmar
weekendEvents.description: A free, family-friendly St. Patrick's Day festival featuring live music from The Elders and Tadhg & Friends, Irish step dancing, a Lucky Clover Market, and festive food and drink from local breweries.
weekendEvents.location: Belmar, 439 S. Upham St. (at Alameda Ave. and Wadsworth Blvd.), Lakewood

weekendEvents.title: Denver Brass: Celtic Mania
weekendEvents.description: Denver Brass presents its annual Celtic Mania program featuring thunderous brass, blazing bagpipes, and electrifying dance alongside Celtic Colorado Pipes & Drums, Rocky Mountain Highland Dancers, and Celtic Steps Irish Dance School. Multiple performances across the weekend; children under 12 are free.
weekendEvents.location: Newman Center for the Performing Arts, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Leadville Ski Joring
weekendEvents.description: A classic Colorado competition in which skiers are towed through a course by horses and riders, navigating jumps, gates, and ring batons on Main Street in Leadville. Free to attend; no dogs allowed.

weekendEvents.title: No Man's Land Film Festival
weekendEvents.description: The premier all-women and gender-nonconforming adventure film festival celebrates athletes and adventurers across sport, exploration, and conservation.
weekendEvents.location: Denver (see festival website for venue details)

weekendEvents.title: Yoga at the Downtown Denver Aquarium
weekendEvents.description: A vinyasa-style yoga class hosted by Yoga Social held inside the Downtown Denver Aquarium, with aquarium admission included in the ticket price.
weekendEvents.location: Downtown Denver Aquarium, 3535 Walnut St., Denver

weekendEvents.title: Demetri Martin: Quick Draw Tour
weekendEvents.description: Stand-up comedian and writer-actor Demetri Martin headlines the Paramount Theatre on his Quick Draw Tour. Tickets range from $52.80 to $66.70.

weekendEvents.title: Molly Brown House Museum Free Admission
weekendEvents.description: In honor of International Women's Day, the Molly Brown House Museum offers free admission. The museum preserves the Victorian home of Margaret 'Molly' Brown, one of Colorado's most celebrated figures.

topStory.headline: Trump Demands Iran's 'Unconditional Surrender' as U.S. Strikes Enter Second Week, Russia Reported Aiding Tehran
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump declared Friday that any resolution to the U.S.-Iran conflict must result in Iran's "unconditional surrender," rejecting the prospect of a negotiated settlement even as Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian confirmed that unnamed countries are pursuing diplomatic mediation efforts. Trump posted the demand on Truth Social, adding that after surrender the U.S. and its allies would work to rebuild Iran economically under new leadership. The statement marks a significant hardening of stated U.S. war objectives and appears to foreclose compromise options that had been signaled by Iran's government.

U.S. Central Command reported that in the first seven days of Operation Epic Fury, American forces have struck more than 3,000 targets and damaged or destroyed 43 Iranian ships. Strikes continued Friday night near Tehran's Mehrabad airport, where Iranian state media and the semiofficial Tasnim news agency reported multiple explosions and large fires visible from the city. The U.S. Embassy in Baghdad issued an emergency alert urging American citizens in Iraq to depart immediately, warning that Iran-aligned militant groups may be planning attacks on Americans at hotels and other locations, as commercial flights in and out of Iraq have ceased.

A serious new complication emerged Friday when four sources told NBC News that Russia is providing Iran with intelligence on the locations of U.S. forces in the Middle East, including the positions of American warships, radar installations, and communication systems. The sources said there is no current indication that Russian intelligence is being used to direct Iranian missile or drone strikes, but the assistance could improve Iran's targeting capabilities. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, in an interview set to air Sunday on CBS News' "60 Minutes," said Trump is "well aware of who's talking to who" and that anything that "shouldn't be happening" is being confronted.

A separate and potentially significant development involves a deadly airstrike on a girls' primary school in the southern Iranian city of Minab on the opening day of the conflict, Feb. 28. U.S. investigators now believe the United States may have been responsible for the strike, which Iranian authorities said killed approximately 180 pupils and staff, including children ages 7 to 12, according to a person familiar with the ongoing inquiry. The assessment is based in part on the fact that U.S. forces were operating in the area while Israeli aircraft were not, though White House spokesperson Anna Kelly said the investigation is ongoing and no final conclusions have been reached. UNICEF has reported that at least 1,332 people have been killed in Iran since the conflict began, including 181 children.

A dozen Senate Democrats, including Minority Leader Chuck Schumer, issued a letter Friday criticizing the strikes as "reckless and poorly conceived" and blaming the conflict for sharp increases in global oil prices. The senators also criticized Trump for temporarily waiving sanctions to allow India to more easily purchase Russian oil, arguing the move enriches Vladimir Putin and undermines pressure on Moscow. Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said Thursday that the U.S. plan for a "clean rapid military victory failed" and warned that any follow-on strategy would also fall short, as Iranian officials publicly maintain readiness for a prolonged conflict.
